fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series III Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: You're in the 20% tax slab, so ELSS gives you tax savings under Section 80C. This fund has delivered strong 5-year returns (21.8%) with high alpha (18.6), meaning it consistently beats the market. It's one of the best tax-saving equity funds available.

fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Flexi Cap Fund Direct Plan -Growth
fund.fund_house: Bank of India Investment Managers Private Limited

allocations.rationale: Flexi cap funds can invest across market sizes, giving the manager freedom to chase the best opportunities. This fund has high alpha (18.3) and strong 3-year returns (23.6%), showing it picks winning stocks. It complements your ELSS by adding diversification and growth potential.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap stocks have historically delivered higher growth than large caps over long periods. This fund has a proven 5-year track record (22.2% returns) and high alpha (13.7), meaning it picks mid-cap winners. It adds growth engine to your portfolio.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Small-cap funds are where the biggest wealth creators hide. This fund has delivered stellar 3-year returns (31.3%) and solid 5-year performance (23.7%). It brings high-growth potential to your portfolio, essential for building a ₹3 Cr corpus.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.sub_category: Equity Scheme - Large & Mid Cap Fund
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund combines large and mid-cap stocks, giving you stability with growth. It has exceptional 3-year returns (26.9%) and the highest alpha (18.6) in its category, meaning it consistently beats the market. It adds another high-conviction growth engine.

fund.name: Nippon India Multi Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Multi cap funds invest across large, mid, and small caps, giving broad exposure. This fund has a long 5-year track record (21.3% returns) and is from a trusted fund house. It adds stability and diversification to your high-growth portfolio.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin by Zerodha if you don't have one.
Step 2: Complete your KYC if not already done.
Step 3: Start monthly SIPs in the six funds listed above with the exact amounts shown.
Step 4: Invest your existing ₹18 Lakh corpus as a lumpsum across these funds in the same 15:25:20:20:10:10 ratio.
Step 5: Set calendar reminders for SIP dates and quarterly reviews.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Confirm all SIPs are running smoothly.
After 1 year: Compare each fund’s performance against its category average. If any fund underperforms for 2 quarters in a row, consider replacing it.
Every 3 months: Review your overall portfolio value and progress toward your ₹3 Cr goal.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio mix. If any category (like small cap) has grown much more than planned (say, above 25% of total), sell some and move the money to underweight categories to bring it back to your original plan. This keeps your risk level steady.
plan.rationale: You're 37 with a 23-year horizon — that’s a huge advantage. Markets grow over time, and you can afford to take smart risks. Your 20% tax slab makes ELSS a no-brainer for tax savings. The rest of your portfolio is built for growth: flexi, mid, small, and large & mid-cap funds have historically delivered the highest returns in India. We’ve avoided debt because at your tax level, even 'safe' debt funds lose value after taxes. This mix gives you the best shot at turning ₹18 lakh + ₹55k/month into ₹3 crore.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will fall temporarily. This is normal. Stay invested.
Small and mid-cap funds are more volatile than large caps. They can fall harder in downturns but grow faster in good times.
Past performance doesn’t guarantee future results. Fund managers can change, and winning strategies can stop working.
ELSS has a 3-year lock-in. You can’t withdraw that money during that period.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase your total SIP by 10% every year to keep pace with salary hikes and inflation. For example, next year, increase from ₹55,000 to ₹60,500/month.

allocation_schedule.trigger: When investment horizon drops to 8 years, reduce equity exposure gradually
Last 3 years before goal, shift to safer assets to protect gains

aps.reasoning: The plan scores highly on long-term alignment (23-year horizon, SIPs) but fails the passive test entirely as it allocates 100% to actively managed funds. The rationales explicitly celebrate high alpha and manager stock-picking capability. Risk metrics (betas near 1 but trailing high tracking errors/IR) confirm active bets. The turnover is moderately high (quarterly reviews, fund replacement if underperforming for 2 quarters). Cost is mentioned (low expense ratios) but it's a minor justification for active fund selection, not the primary driver. Research emphasis is minimal; the plan relies on broad market conviction via category diversification rather than individual stock analysis.

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows strong tax efficiency for a 20% slab investor, correctly utilizing ELSS and avoiding slab-taxed debt funds. The diversified equity allocation across six funds is adequate, though the concentration in three funds (65%) triggers a slight penalty for lack of fund-house diversification. Goal alignment is excellent for the long-term (23-year) horizon but the 'all-equity' strategy creates a miss on risk-appropriateness; a moderately aggressive investor needs some debt buffer to survive bear markets, and total equity is too volatile for a plan promising only 15.7% returns. The plan is internally consistent in its logic (avoiding debt due to taxes) but this logic is flawed for a moderate investor.
